title: PROPS is not a rotation-frame selector — the ROM draws ALL THREE blade pairs every frame
claim: topology.ts:161 documents PROPS as 'the three prop-blade frames, indexed by prop rotation'. The ROM's PLNPRP loop does the opposite: it sets Y=4, calls DECMAP on PROPS[Y], resets the pen origin, then DEY/DEY and BPL back — three iterations (Y=4,2,0), commented ';DO ALL THREE BLADE PAIRS'. PPROPA, PPROPB and PPROPC are all decoded into the SAME picture on EVERY frame, producing a static 6-spoke blur, not a 3-frame animation.
reasoning: RBARON.MAC:5052-5066. The spin illusion for the enemy plane's prop does not come from cycling PROPS; the prop POINT-SET is re-rotated each frame by PLTEST (LDX I,1 selects DBPROP in the PPNTS table) and all three blade pairs are always drawn. The animated PLAYER prop is a different asset entirely — the six JMPL pictures behind `.PROPS`/LNEPRP (037007.XXX:70-75). Our data is correct (OB-009); only the semantics attached to it are wrong, and any consumer that reads PROPS as a frame index will animate a prop the original never animated. PROPS currently has no consumer in src/, so this is a comment + usage-contract fix before it acquires one.

ours.verbatim: export const PROPS: readonly (readonly ConnectOp[])[] = [PPROPA, PPROPB, PPROPC]

title: The LOD switch is an orientation STATE BIT, not a camera distance — LOD_DISTANCE = 1500 is invented
claim: biplane.ts selects the 42-vertex model vs. the 29-vertex drone with `depth < LOD_DISTANCE` where LOD_DISTANCE = 1500 (a number that appears nowhere in the ROM). The ROM's DRNPIC selects on bit $10 (D4) of PLSTAT+6: `LDA PLSTAT+6 ;PLANE ROTATED / AND I,10 / BEQ 20$`. D4=0 ('PLANE FACING AWAY', set at RBARON.MAC:2650-2652) takes branch 20$ -> `.DRPNT` (29 points), PLNABZ only (';2-D DRONE POINTS', Z-axis rotation alone) -> decode DB.MAR. D4=1 falls through -> DROFFZ scale-down + DRNTST -> decode DB.MAP (the full 42-vertex list, falling through into DB.MAR).
reasoning: RBARON.MAC:4961-4979. The criterion is whether the plane needs a 3-D rotation at all: a drone flying directly away from the player is rendered by a cheap 2-D (Z-only) transform of the front 29 points; a drone that has rotated out of that pose gets the full 42-point 3-D path. Distance never enters the branch. Our threshold therefore switches LOD at the wrong times: a near plane facing away keeps its back faces (the ROM drops them), and a far plane mid-turn loses them (the ROM keeps them). Fix: drive biplaneLOD from the enemy's facing/rotation state rather than from projected depth. Size m because the LOD entry point and every caller's argument change (enemy.ts supplies depth today), and the tests that pin LOD_DISTANCE move with it.

title: findings.md calls the LOD a 'distance LOD'; the ROM has no distance test
claim: docs/red-baron-1980-source-findings.md:321 asserts a 'Built-in distance LOD'. There is no distance comparison anywhere in the plane/drone picture path. The selector is PLSTAT+6 bit $10 (see OB-012). The doc's split itself (42 pts + DB.MAP + DB.LNS near; 29 pts + DB.MAR far) is correct — only the word 'distance' is fabricated, and biplane.ts inherited it as LOD_DISTANCE = 1500.
reasoning: This is the secondary-source trap doing exactly what it does: our code cites the doc as authority (biplane.ts:106-110 even says 'the ROM pins the near-full/far-drone SPLIT but not the switch distance'), when in fact the ROM pins the switch CRITERION precisely — it is simply not a distance. The doc should say 'orientation LOD (PLSTAT+6 D4, PLANE FACING AWAY)'. Correcting the prose is what unblocks OB-012 from recurring.

ours.verbatim: distance LOD:** the *full* plane = 42 pts (`.PLPNT`); the *drone / distant* plane = **29 pts** (`.DRPNT`,

title: The wing struts (DB.LNS) are drawn DIMMER than the airframe; renderModel has no intensity at all
claim: PLDECD decodes DB.MAP (-> DB.MAR) at the full brightness V.BRIT, then drops the intensity by $60 (floored at 0) via `SBC I,60` / `BCS 10$` / `LDA I,0` and issues a VGSTAT before decoding DB.LNS — the comment is ';ADD LIGHTER LINES'. Our renderModel emits a flat readonly SceneSegment[] of {x1,y1,x2,y2} with no intensity field, so all 84 ops of [...DB_MAP, ...DB_MAR, ...DB_LNS] render at one brightness.
reasoning: RBARON.MAC:5026-5040. On a real vector monitor the inter-plane bracing wires are visibly fainter than the fuselage and wings — that two-tier brightness is a large part of why the original biplane reads as a biplane and not a wireframe blob. We have the correct 17 strut ops (OB-003) but throw away the intensity that distinguishes them. Size m: SceneSegment (src/core/scene.ts:21-26) has no intensity member, so the field, projectSegment's construction of it, and the shell renderer that consumes it all move together.

ours.verbatim: export function renderModel(model: BiplaneModel, mvp: Mat4): readonly SceneSegment[] {

title: Every RBARON.MAC line citation in biplane.ts points at the wrong line
claim: biplane.ts cites the plane point table as 'RBARON.MAC:6206-6259' (line 10) and 'RBARON.MAC:6212-6256' (lines 36, 41); DB.PLN..P.BACK actually spans RBARON.MAC:6221-6265. It cites `.PLPNT` at :6258 (line 14) and `.DRPNT` at :6259 (line 90); they are at :6267 and :6268. It cites the POINTP macro at 'RBARON.MAC:57' (line 11); it is at :29. The findings doc's ':6207-6279' (line 320) is likewise off.
reasoning: The VALUES transcribed are all correct (OB-004) — this is anchor drift, not data drift, and it is uniformly 5-9 lines short, which is what you get when line numbers are copied from a differently-lineated file rather than re-read from RBARON.MAC. It still matters: these anchors are the only audit trail a future maintainer has for a 42-line hand-copied table, and a citation that lands on the wrong line is a citation nobody can check. Re-anchor to 6221/6253/6267/6268/29.

ours.verbatim: // PLANE POINTS DB — the 42 biplane vertices (RBARON.MAC:6212-6256).

title: BLCOLL — the blimp's 8-vertex collision box — is never transcribed
claim: BLCOLL (RBARON.MAC:6270-6277) is 8 POINTP vertices: (16,16,-40) (16,-16,-40) (-16,16,-40) (-16,-16,-40) (16,16,40) (16,-16,40) (-16,16,40) (-16,-16,40) — a +/-16, +/-16, +/-40 box — with `.BLCL = .-BLCOLL-3` = 21. CDSSET uses it for the blimp hit test (`BIT BLOBJ+18 ;BLIMP ?` ... `LDY I,.BLCL` ... `JSR MNMX0 ;DETERMINE MIN/MAX OF X,Y&Z`). It appears in the PLNDB master table (RBARON.MAC:6285-6287) alongside DB.PLN/DBPROP/COLLD/PIECE0-3/BLIMP/STAR0/STAR1 — every other member of which IS in topology.ts. BLCOLL is the only omission.
reasoning: topology.ts transcribes COLLD (the plane's collision window) but not BLCOLL (the blimp's). Our findings doc even names BLCOLL at line 323 and describes it correctly — it just never landed in code, so blimp.ts has no ROM-derived hit volume. The ROM rotates the 8 corners (PLNABZ + YAXIS) and takes their min/max, i.e. it is an ORIENTED box reduced to an AABB per frame, not a static AABB. Size s: 8 triples plus an export.

title: The four ground-target vector objects (pyramid, house, tank, pill box) are entirely absent
claim: 037007.XXX:1132-1230 defines a complete, self-contained object set that topology.ts does not contain at all: point-sets PFPYRM (4 PFPNTS), PFHOME (7), PFTANK (10), PFPBOX (16); decode-lists DEPFPY (5 ops), DEPFHS (8), DEPFTK (11), DEPFPB (18); the PFODEC pointer table (`.WORD DEPFPY,DEPFHS,DEPFTK,DEPFPB`), the PFLOB point-set table (`.WORD PFPYRM,PFHOME,PFTANK,PFPBOX`), the `.PFLOB` length table, and the PFOFFS table of 12 PFCOL screen offsets (037007.XXX:1232-1246).
reasoning: These are vector OBJECT data in the same POINTP/BV/VV vocabulary as everything else in this pair, and they are the ground targets the player strafes — the objects PFOCOL's collision boxes (already in topology.ts:371-384) exist to hit. We shipped the collision boxes without the things they collide with. Note the point macro here is PFPNTS, which emits only two bytes (.X/2, .Y*2) and DISCARDS its third argument — so these are planar billboards, and the constant 32/24/28/34 third column in the source is inert (topology.ts:296-302 already documents PFPNTS correctly for the SCAPEs). Size m: four point-sets, four decode-lists, two pointer tables, plus tests.

title: The SLAP** mountain-highlight lists and the SCLMAP table are absent and unmentioned
claim: 037007.XXX:258-307 defines ten SLAP** connect-lists (SLAP01, SLAP02, SLAP03, SLAP11, SLAP13, SLAP20, SLAP21, SLAP22, SLAP30, SLAP32) and 037007.XXX:321-324 defines SCLMAP, a 16-entry pointer table into them with -1 in the six empty slots (RBARON.MAC:403 names it ';PF OBJECT HIGHLIGHT DECODE POINTERS'). topology.ts contains neither, and no comment in the repo acknowledges they exist. Separately, the 16 reverse-scroll SMP** lists (037007.XXX:175-256) are absent but ARE acknowledged as deferred (topology.ts:412).
reasoning: topology.ts:399-414 presents the mountain render data as 'PFOPOS transcribed in full + the 16 forward SMAP lists', with only the SMP** reverse set called out as deferred. That inventory is incomplete: a THIRD list family (SLAP**, the highlight/second-intensity pass over the same SCAPE points, reached via SCMAP+40) is missing without acknowledgement, so the file reads as complete when it is not. SMP** is a known deferral; SLAP**/SCLMAP is an unknown one, which is the more dangerous kind. Landscape-adjacent, but it lives in my file, so it is reported here.
